explanation_md: `x > 0 && x < 10` is true (1). `x < 0 || x > 3` is true because `x > 3` is true (1). `!(x == 5)` is `!true` which is false (0).
`counter != 0` is false, so `&&` short-circuits and never evaluates `++counter`. `counter` remains 0. The result is false (0). Learners who expect `++counter` to always run will guess `counter == 1`.

prompt_md: Which expression correctly tests whether `n` is outside the range [1, 100] (i.e., strictly below 1 or strictly above 100)?
explanation_md: A number is outside [1, 100] when it is less than 1 OR greater than 100 — these are two separate conditions joined by OR. Option a uses AND, which can never be true (no number is simultaneously less than 1 and greater than 100). Options c and d apply `!` incorrectly.

prompt_md: Which expression is equivalent to `!(a || b)` according to De Morgan's laws?
explanation_md: De Morgan's law states `!(a || b)` equals `!a && !b`: negate the whole expression, flip OR to AND, and negate each operand. Option a (`!a || !b`) is De Morgan's expansion of `!(a && b)`, not `!(a || b)`.

options.a: The program uses `&&` where it should use `||`.
options.b: The safety check `divisor != 0` appears on the right side of `&&`, so `numerator / divisor` is evaluated first, causing division by zero before the guard runs.
options.c: The comparison `numerator / divisor > 0` should use `>=` to include zero.
options.d: The bug is that `divisor` is declared as `int` rather than `double`.

explanation_md: Short-circuit evaluation only skips the RIGHT operand when the LEFT is false. Here the division `numerator / divisor` is evaluated first, before the safety check, causing undefined behavior (division by zero) for `divisor == 0`. The fix is to swap the operands: `(divisor != 0) && (numerator / divisor > 0)`.
